sections.paragraph:
an unused verb, of which the native power may be pretty certainly gathered from the derivatives and cognate roots, and which also extends into the Indo-Germanic languages. It is-

(1) prop. to extend; compare in the Phœnicio-Shemitic languages, Eth. ቲን፡ length, נָתַן and תָּנָה to give, prop. to stretch out the hand (compare יָד, יָדָה), יָתַן to extend itself (as time), to endure, to be continual; in the Indo-Germanic languages, Sanscr. tan, Gr. τείνω, τανύω, τιταίνω, Lat. tendo (compare Dissert. Lugdd. ii. 852); whence tenuis (Sanscr. tanu), Goth. thanjan, Germ. dehnen, with many words which have affinity with these, as the old High Germ. Tanna, fir tree. Hence תַּנִּין vast fish (th’to~), so called from the length to which it extends, compare ταινία (from τείνω) a long fish, tœnia.

(2) It is applied to contention in running, as being done with outstretched neck, like the Gr. τάνυμι; hence תַּן jackal, so called from its swiftness in running.

II.

תנן

Syr. and Ch. to smoke. Hence אַתּוּן for אַתְנוּן furnace, and תַּנּוּר from תַּן and נוּר. [Compare the Welsh, tân, fire.]
